entry.title: Must-See Utilities Highlights at SAP Sapphire Madrid
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

SAP Sapphire kicked off last week in Orlando where SAP CEO Christian Klein announced the Business AI Platform as the foundation for the Autonomous Enterprise,  enabling AI agents to run end‑to‑end business processes with deep business context, governance, and security. SAP’s vision goes beyond traditional ERP by embedding AI directly into mission‑critical workflows, helping organizations turn AI investments into tangible, accurate, and scalable business outcomes.

Explore the SAP Sapphire 2026 news and innovations and watch keynotes on demand. 

 

This week, SAP Sapphire continues in Madrid. While the core content remains similar, discussions often take on a more European perspective, with a stronger focus on regulation, regional market dynamics, and local customer success stories.
 
There are many exciting sessions—I'd especially like to highlight three Utilities-specific highlights, all taking place on Wednesday, May 20.

Ignite the future: Accelerate your energy transition | IND1261 at 10:30 CEST in Hall 9, Industries Center, Theater 

The global race toward energy transition presents not only complex challenges but also unprecedented opportunities for industry leaders and consumers. SAP’s groundbreaking innovations, real-world AI applications, and fresh partnerships are reshaping the landscape. Thrive in a rapidly evolving market and be at the forefront of the energy revolution. Join Filip Kowalski's (MEE Industry Lead for Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy) presentation. 

National Grid transforms capital investment accounting with SAP BTP and AI | BTP1483 at 1:00 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 10

Discover how agentic AI is enabling the next generation of capital investment accounting with SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and AI. Hear how AI-powered agents at British energy company National Grid plc help autonomously orchestrate processes, improve financial transparency, and support faster, more-informed investment decisions. The session will be presented by Aley Matthewson, SAP Functional Manager at National Grid and Blake Goddard, Senior Business AI Platform Executive at SAP. 

Industry AI: Reimagining core processes across every industry | IND1407 at 1:30 in Hall 10, Theater 11

Across industries, businesses have the same goal: find ways to use AI that actually deliver on its promise. See what next-generation industry innovation looks like and how SAP helps customers execute on it. Discover two new use cases codeveloped with pharmaceutical packaging leader Uhlmann Group and premier professional services firm PwC.
Speakers: Robert Brachman, Head fo Business Engineering, Uhlmann Pac-Systeme; Jill Notz, MX Controller, PwC; Thomas Ruhl, CPO Industries, SAP; Andre Bechthold, President SAP Industries & Experiences, SAP; 

RWE: AI-driven offshore wind troubleshooting | IND1609 at 3:30 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 14 

RWE uses AI in SAP solutions to speed offshore wind repairs and boost asset uptime. Technicians deploy AI-assisted SAP solutions to analyze past notes and OEM manuals, pinpoint root causes, and draft tailored repair work orders. Field technicians get support to improve repair documentation and feed insights into the system for continuous learning.

Join the session from Frank Scholtka, Director Operations UK at RWE Offshore Wind GmbH and Bruno Pincolini, Global Lead for SAP Business AI for Utilities at SAP.

 
Join us also at the Utilities Industry Expert Station in Hall 9 (Industries Center – IND1520) to connect 1:1 with SAP specialists and explore your key challenges and opportunities. Discover how AI is reshaping industry processes at the Experience Hub (EXP1536), and gain valuable insights into SAP’s portfolio during the Utilities & Oil & Gas Industry Tour. Reach out to your SAP Account Manager to reserve your spot and make the most of these tailored experiences.

Visit the SAP Sapphire Madrid website to explore topics and build your personal schedule. 

I am looking forward to hearing your feedback.

Best regards,

Nicole

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP

Introduction

Most of the utilities organization did not build batch-driven systems because they were wrong. They built them because, for many years, batch processing was enough and serving the business need to generate the mass customer bills and satisfying other business processes like payment processing, return and refunds etc.

Meter reads come daily. Billing jobs ran overnight. Outage analysis could wait a few minutes. But now with the new AI Era this world is changing fast.

That’s means today, utilities operate in an environment where:

  • Smart meters generate continuous streams of events.
  • EV charging creates unpredictable load spikes,
  • Distributed energy resources change grid behavior dynamically,
  • Customers expect instant updates and near real-time visibility,

In this environment, delays are no longer just technical limitations — they directly impact operations and customer experience.

Let’s understand this with a simple example occurs daily.

A customer loses power at 2:05 PM, the smart meter detects the outage immediately.

But the utility system processes that information at 2:30 PM, the question is why and the answer is because the event is sitting inside a scheduled batch cycle. Now that 25-minute delay creates:

  • Slower outage response.
  • Increased call center traffic.
  • Poor customer experience.
  • Potential regulatory exposure.

This is where event-driven architecture becomes critical. And this is where SAP BTP can play a major role in modern utility architecture.

This is exactly where event-driven architecture comes in—and where SAP BTP starts to make a real difference.

1.       Why Event-Driven Utilities Matter (In Real Life)

Utilities are no longer operating predictable, centralized grids.

The grid is becoming:

  • Distributed
  • Dynamic
  • High-volume
  • Event-heavy

Millions of devices are continuously generating signals:

  • Smart meters
  • Grid sensors
  • DER systems
  • EV charging stations
  • Field devices

Traditional batch systems struggle in this environment — not because the technology is bad, but because the operating model has changed.

Event-driven architecture changes the model completely.

That’s means Instead of waiting for scheduled processing windows or batch, systems react when events occur. In simple terms real time processing windows.

That enables:

  • Faster outage detection
  • Near real-time billing updates
  • Automated operational workflows
  • Immediate exception handling
  • Better grid visibility

This shift is not only about speed. It is about building utilities that can respond continuously instead of periodically.

2.       Where SAP BTP Fits In (Simple View)

Think of SAP BTP Event Mesh as the traffic controller for events. In many utilities, integration landscapes become tightly coupled over time.

One system talks directly to another.  Then another connection gets added. Then another.

Eventually, even small changes become difficult and risky.

This is where SAP Event Mesh on SAP BTP becomes important.

Think of Event Mesh as the event backbone of the enterprise.

Instead of systems communicating directly:

  • Systems publish events
  • Interested systems subscribe to them
  • Processing becomes decoupled and scalable

In a utility environment:

  • AMI systems publish meter events
  • Grid applications publish operational events
  • SAP S/4HANA consumes only business-relevant events
  • Other BTP services enrich, filter, or analyze the data

This creates flexibility that traditional point-to-point integration cannot provide.

But there is an important architectural reality here:

Poor event design creates the same problems as poor API design.

If event topics, queue structures, and filtering rules are not designed carefully, Event Mesh can quickly become another bottleneck.

3.       A Simple End-to-End Example (AMI → BTP → S/4HANA)

Let’s walk through a simple but real-time business case or scenario.

Scenario of Power outage detected

  • Step 1 — Meter detects outage: A smart meter immediately detects loss of power.
  • Smart meter sends an outage event immediately.
  • This goes to SAP BTP (via MQTT or API).
  • At this point, no batch job is waiting to process the information later. The event is available immediately.

 

  • Step 2 — SAP BTP Processes the Event

From there:

  • Integration services validate the payload.
  • Business rules evaluate the event.
  • Additional context is added.

For example:

  • Is this a single-home outage?
  • Or are multiple meters reporting outages from the same feeder?

This is where intelligence starts to matter. BTP services can:

  • Correlate events,
  • Filter noise
  • Detect patterns
  • Trigger automation

In many architectures, this becomes the real operational brain of the utility platform.

  • Step 3 — SAP S/4HANA Executes the Business Process

 Once the event is validated:

  • A work order can be created automatically
  • Field crews can be dispatched
  • Customers can receive notifications
  • Device management processes can begin
  • Billing or service workflows can update

S/4HANA remains the system of record. But SAP BTP handles the real-time orchestration layer around it. That separation is important for scalability.

4.        What Actually Changes (Batch vs Real-Time)

Traditional Batch Model

  • Meter data processed hourly or daily
  • Exceptions reviewed manually
  • Outage visibility delayed
  • Billing corrections handled later
  • Integrations tightly coupled

Event-Driven Model

  • Continuous event processing
  • Automated workflow triggering
  • Near real-time outage detection
  • Dynamic operational response
  • Scalable publish/subscribe integrations

But there is an important operational truth many projects have discovered late:

Real-time systems expose process problems that batch systems quietly hide.

When systems react immediately:

  • Data quality issues surface faster, and this is real point of having BTP.
  • Business rule conflicts become visible, immediate effect/result.
  • Operational inefficiencies can no longer stay hidden behind overnight processing.

This is why event-driven transformation is both technical and organizational.

5.       Where Things Usually Go Wrong (From Experience)

Mostly in real projects, challenges show up quickly:

  • Event storms: Millions of AMI events can flood the platform during outages or restoration cycles. Without proper throttling and filtering, downstream systems become overwhelmed.
  • Poor event design: If event topics are designed inconsistently:
    • Consumers become tightly coupled
    • Governance becomes difficult
    • Reusability disappears
  • Overloading S/4HANA: Not every event belongs inside ERP. One of the biggest mistakes is forwarding raw operational noise directly into S/4HANA. ERP should receive business-relevant outcomes — not every technical signal.
  • Duplicate processing: In distributed event systems, duplicate events are normal. Architectures must be designed with idempotency in mind from the beginning.

And the biggest one:

Business rules have become more complex than expected. In most implementations, BTP becomes the brain, not just the pipe.

6.        Architecture Principle That Matters Most

The biggest mindset about this modern architecture shift is this:

SAP BTP should not behave like traditional middleware.

And it should be important to understand since most of the client leadership want new system but same like old. It should behave like:

  • An orchestration platform
  • An event intelligence layer
  • A real-time decision platform

That is a very different architectural role.

7.       Key Patterns That Actually Work

From a practitioner point of view, these patterns matter:

  • Use publish/subscribe, not point-to-point integrations
  • Filter and enrich events before they reach S/4HANA
  • Keep S/4HANA focused on business transactions, not raw events
  • Design for idempotency (avoid duplicates)
  • Design events for reuse and scalability
  • Treat Event Mesh as a platform capability, not just middleware

8.       Final Thought

Important things we need to understand as architects and as organization leaders, moving to event-driven architecture is not simply a technology modernization effort. It will change how utilities operate, most of the way business processes execute may change or enhance. That’s result an organization becoming:

  • Faster decisions which will improve business process.
  • Better customer experience.
  • More automation, less manual work.
  • Less dependency on batch cycles.

But it also requires discipline in design. Because one system reacts instantly:


Its means There is very little room for poorly designed processes, noisy integrations, or unclear ownership.

In real-time utilities, architecture quality becomes operational quality.

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #3

In the next article, we move closer to the operational grid layer:

  • OT/IT convergence
  • MQTT ingestion patterns
  • IEC 61850 integration
  • Substation-to-cloud flows
  • Security and isolation models.

This is where utility architecture starts moving from enterprise latency… to grid latency.

entry.title: Create a Custom SAP BDC Data Product share it with Enterprise Databricks, extend it and send it back
entry.content:
Overview:

This blog demonstrates how to leverage SAP Business Data Catalog (BDC) in conjunction with Enterprise Databricks to create custom data products, extend them with advanced analytics and transformations, and synchronize the refined data back to SAP BDC. This integrated approach enables organizations to maximize data value while maintaining governance and traceability across their data infrastructure.

Main steps at a glance

1. Check the prerequisites

a. Enable your SAP BDC Core in SAP4Me portal, it important to provision it in the same hyperscaler and region of your Enterprise Databricks or in the future your GCP or Microsoft Fabric tenants, because you may incur some egress fees when you share data products through a BDC Connect instance. You can refer to this blog Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Provision your SAP Datasphere tenat in SAP BDC through SAP4Me you can refer to this blog Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Datasphere  when you provision at least allocate 4,150 CU because you need to activate object store in you tenant, so you need to increase the storage to 384 GB and memory to 128 GB and in the object store you need to add 1 block for storage, 1 block for memory and 1 block for requests you can use the SAP Business Data Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator to configure your business case

c. Create one HANA Data Lake Files space in you Datasphere tenant, and add it to an scope role to provide access

d. Provision your SAP BDC Connect instance in SAP4Me portal, you can check this blog How to provision SAP BDC Connect for Databricks?

e. Prepare your source system with all the configuration needed to connect with you SAP Datasphere tenant, in this document you can check all the connection types and prerequisites -> SAP Datasphere Connection Types

f. If you want to connect an S4HANA system then you need to configure your cloud connector for S4HANA PCE or S4HANA On-Premise connection with SAP Datasphere:

  • Step 1: Setup SAP Cloud Connector connect to SAPDatasphere
  • Step 2: Setup mapping (RFC connection) in Cloud Connector for a SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection
  • Step 3: Create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Data Flow and Replication Flow
  • Step 4: Replication Flow for CDS views from S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Datasphere

2. Create the formation

a. Create your formation in SAP4Me portal. Go to Portfolio & Products -> My Product Packages tab-> Business Data Cloud product package -> Customer Landscape tab -> Formations sub-tab and choose Create Formation, enter a unique formation name and choose Integration with SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Include the following mandatory systems: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP BDC Connect instance. It's not mandatory to include your source system in the formation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. Create custom managed data product

a. Open your SAP Datasphere tenant. First of all replicate your data. You can replicate directly to object store creating a connection from your source system to SAP Datasphere in the HANA Data Lake Files space or you can replicate an existing table coming from another space in your Datasphere tenant. If you want to use a view or analytic model you must materialize the view or model in a table, then replicate into a table in your HANA Data Lake Files space. My table is TL_ADW_OBSESSION

b. Create a formation profile in your SAP Datasphere tenant. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Provider Profile -> Create Data Provider Profile. In this example my data provider profile is ONB_Demo

 

c. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Products -> Click on Create Product (+ icon)

d. In Artifact Space select the object store space for your data products

e. Select your table in my case is TL_ADW_OBSESSION and click on Save Changes

f. Click on Switch Status and select List. When the data product is Listed please open it

g. Open your SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit and go to Catalog & Marketplace, click on Search and we look for ONB_DEMO data product, open it and click on Share

h. Click on Add Target. Select your BDC Connect instance and click on Share

4. Extend and push it back to SAP BDC

a. In our SAP Datasphere tenant we select the space which can install data products. Go to System -> Configuration -> Business Data Products

b. Select your BDC Connect Instance and in the left side click on Add to select the space where you want to install data products

c. Open your Enterprise Databricks workspace and go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing

d. Click on your BDC Connect provider

e. Click on Mount to Catalog

f. Provide the catalog name and click on create

                                 

 

 

 

 

g. Go back to your Catalog and in Delta Shares Received you can see bdc_deltashare catalog with the table coming from Datasphere TL_ADW_OBSESSION

h. Extend the table with advanced analytics and/or transformations. Then new table in Databricks is cluster_final, the next step is create a share object. Go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab and click Share data

i. Create a share page, enter the share Name and add an optional comment. Click on Save and continue

j. On the Add data assets tab, select the data assets you want to share. In this case I want to share the view cluster_final. Then click on Save and continue

k. If is the first time you share assets maybe the system will ask to grant some required permissions, click on Confirm

l. In the next step select the notebooks you want to share. In this case I don't want it. Click on Save and continue

m. In the final step select your BDC Connect provider as recipient. Then click on Share data

n. Now we have a share bdc_connect with this we are sharing the view cluster_final

o. Then please go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab, click Recipients and select the recipient

p. Click on Grant share. On the Grant share dialog, start typing the share name or click the drop-down menu to select the shares you want to grant, finally click on Grant

q. Finally you need to publishing the share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud. The next image is an example about the last step to publish, please follow this documentation -> Publishing the Delta Share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud.  for the complete process and steps

r. Then we come back to SAP BDC Cockpit and we should be able to see our new data product coming from Enterprise Databricks

Summary:

In this blog, we explore how to create a custom data product in SAP Datasphere, the steps to share it with an SAP BDC Connect instance, extend it in Databrick by creating a new cluster table and finally, send it back to SAP Business Data Cloud as a new data product.

entry.title: Re: while trying to replication the ABCO Transaction code...
entry.content:
The error occurs because the asset is classified as an investment measure, and manual postings to such assets are not permitted.

  • You are not allowed to post transactions that affect acquisition value using the current transaction.

So either :

  • Use ABCO
  • Look at some notes such as 611586 - AK005 with adjustment postings to AuC which will allow Customizing of the error message

entry.title: Re: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Great write-up, @Atul_Joshi85. Section 5 is painfully accurate especially the trap of overloading S/4HANA. I’ve seen way too many projects try to dump every single raw AMI ping straight into the ERP instead of letting BTP do the heavy lifting to filter the noise. S/4HANA needs actual business events, not raw telemetry!

Also, completely agree on real-time exposing all the bad data and process issues that batch jobs used to quietly sweep under the rug overnight. Making the jump to event-driven forces a level of data discipline that almost always catches clients off guard on day one.

Looking forward to part 3. OT/IT convergence and MQTT ingestion is where things get really interesting.

entry.title: Re: Configurando um imposto para não gerar movimentação c...
entry.content:
Olá Gustavo, 
Primeiramente obrigada pela disposição em ajudar. 

Logo após esse post, a resposta 'veio'. Na tela de Definição de Utilização, selecionei a opção 'Gratuito' (vinculada ao Parceiro de Negócios) para a utilização correspondente. Ao testar a aplicação no Pedido de Compra, o item foi processado corretamente e não gerou movimentação financeira, atendendo perfeitamente ao comportamento esperado.



Grata;

entry.title: Re: SAC: Measure-Based Dimension vs Calculated Dimension
entry.content:
Hi Egil,

Honestly, the easiest way to handle this without overcomplicating things is just adding a standard measure filter directly on the Geo Map widget (Revenue > 50,000).

But to address your title about MBD vs Calculated Dimensions:

If this is a permanent rule and you just want the smaller bubbles gone, create a Calculated Measure: IF([Revenue] > 50000, [Revenue], NULL). SAC ignores the nulls, so they won't render on the map at all.

If you want your users to be able to filter the map dynamically later on, definitely go with a Measure-Based Dimension. You can group the customers into tiers and drop it in as an input control.

Hope that helps!

entry.title: Re: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:

If you’re working with similar scenarios, I’d be interested to hear how your teams are approaching:

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA orchestration

I’m planning to expand this series with deeper technical patterns and real‑world examples, so any insights from your side will help shape the next article.

entry.title: Re: How to Trace a Role That Appears Blank in PFCG but Still Controls IT0008 Access?
entry.content: I have no idea. If nobody answers, start an SQL trace, maintain PA30/IT0008, stop the trace, display the trace, look for the table AGR_DEFINE (role master table) or AGR_1016 (role-profile) or possibly any other AGR*, UST* or USR* tables, and you'll see the code which checks if a given role is assigned to the current user. Otherwise, start the trace STAUTHTRACE.

entry.title: Table Widget Improvements in SAP Analytics Cloud 2026 Q2 QRC
entry.content:
The table as the workhorse of analytics

In SAP Analytics Cloud, the table widget remains one of the most used and most relied-upon objects in any story. It is where numbers are compared, decisions are justified, and analysis is communicated.

With this 2026 Q2 QRC release, we are introducing two new capabilities that extend what tables can do. The first addresses a long-standing limitation in cross-system reporting: the ability to display complete, reliable totals in tables that combine data from multiple models. The second gives analytics developers a new degree of programmatic control over how hierarchy data is presented to end users at runtime.

Both improvements are independent. Both solve real problems. Together, they reflect a continued investment in making the table widget more capable, more flexible, and more trustworthy.

 

One table, multiple models, complete totals

Many organizations run their analytics across more than one data source. Actuals may come from one system, budgets from another. A single report often needs to bring these together in one place.

Until now, this created a specific and frustrating gap: when a table in SAP Analytics Cloud combined data from blended models, totals were not available. The aggregated row - the one that gives a report its summary, its bottom line - was simply missing. Users had to work around it, accept incomplete views, or maintain separate reports.

This capability closes that gap. Tables and charts built on blended data models can now display totals. These totals update dynamically as filters are applied, so the summary row always reflects the current view of the data.

In practice, this means a financial dashboard that pulls actuals from one system and budget data from another can now show a consolidated total row, just as any single-model table would. The report is complete. The numbers add up. The trust is there.

This is especially relevant for financial reporting, executive dashboards, and any scenario where business decisions depend on seeing the full picture across data sources - not just the detail rows.

What this capability delivers

  • Display totals in blended tables and charts
  • Totals update dynamically as filters are applied
  • Supports SAP S/4HANA and SAP BW/4HANA blended scenarios
  • Supports subquery measures and accounts with base aggregation: SUM, MIN, MAX, NO NULL, NO ZERO
  • Tooltip guidance when a measure is not eligible for totals

Limitations

This feature requires Optimized Story Designer with the new table build experience. Backend version dependencies apply: SAP S/4HANA QRC3 2025 (EPM 2025.28) or later; SAP BW/4HANA minimum 7.50 SP33.

 

Hierarchy display, on your terms

For business users

Hierarchical data is common in planning and reporting. Organizational structures, account hierarchies, cost center trees - these are the backbone of many analytical models. But not every use case calls for the full hierarchy to be visible.

Sometimes, the right view is the full tree: all levels, all members, full context. Other times, only the leaf members matter - the actionable line items, the individual accounts, the specific cost centers where data is entered or reviewed.

In the past, this choice was made at design time and stayed fixed. A table showed what it was configured to show. Changing the display required going back into the story designer.

With this improvement, that choice can now be made at runtime. Analytics designers can build stories where the hierarchy display adapts dynamically - switching between the full hierarchy and a leaves-only view based on user interaction or application logic. The end user experiences a cleaner, more focused table when they need it, without losing access to the full structure when context requires it.

For analytics developers

This capability is delivered as an extension of the existing setHierarchy() scripting function. A new optional parameter - HierarchyMode.LeavesOnly - has been added, which toggles the “Show Only Leaves” mode for a hierarchical dimension on a table widget at runtime.

The parameter is optional and backward-compatible. Existing scripts using setHierarchy() are unaffected.

A typical implementation: a planning application where users switch between an input view - showing only leaf members for data entry - and a review view - showing the full hierarchy for aggregation and context. A single script call handles the transition. No duplication of widgets, no workarounds, no loss of planning integrity.

This is currently supported on UQM table widgets. It is delivered as part of setHierarchy() and does not extend to other widget types such as charts or geomaps.

What this capability delivers

  • New optional parameter HierarchyMode.LeavesOnly added to the existing setHierarchy() scripting function
  • Toggles “Show Only Leaves” mode for a hierarchical dimension on a table widget at runtime
  • Fully backward-compatible - existing scripts using setHierarchy() are unaffected
  • Provides a clean, non-destructive way to surface only actionable leaf members, while keeping the hierarchy structure intact for writeback and aggregation

Limitations

Supported on UQM table widgets only. Not available for ListTable, Chart, Geomap, or other widget types.

 

Share your feedback

These two improvements represent targeted additions to one of the most central objects in SAP Analytics Cloud. We hope they address real needs you have encountered in your own reporting and development work.

That said, we want to hear from you. How are you using blended models today? What hierarchy display challenges have you run into in planning applications? Your feedback directly shapes how we continue to evolve the table widget.

Please share your thoughts in the comments. Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Refreshing Integration Card Data on Dialog Close from Shell Plugin in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working on SAP Build Work Zone (Advanced Edition), where I have implemented a Shell Plugin that opens a custom dialog.

Requirement:
After the dialog is closed, I need to trigger a refresh in an Integration Card that is present on the Work Zone Home screen. This refresh should result in the card fetching the latest data via its configured API.

I am looking for guidance on how to properly establish communication between a Shell Plugin and an Integration Card in this scenario.

Questions:

  • What is the recommended way to trigger or notify an Integration Card to refresh from a Shell Plugin?

  • Are there any supported communication mechanisms between Shell Plugins and Integration Cards in Work Zone Advanced?

  • Is there a standard approach to externally trigger a card’s data refresh or API call?

  • How are such cross-component interactions typically handled in Work Zone architecture?

Additional Context:
The Integration Card is developed using a standard MVC approach, with separate view and controller files handling the presentation and logic.

Any guidance, best practices, or references would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Combined Credit/Debit Note Posting Against Multiple Invoices for Customer Rate Difference
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We are currently using the Customer Rate Difference Bills Auto Debit process in SAP GROW / SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

The existing process is working as expected. However, we have a new business requirement for selected customers.

Current Process:
Credit notes and debit notes are generated based on POAM / rate difference adjustments, and the current SAP GROW process is functioning correctly.

New Requirement:
For a few customers, instead of posting separate credit and debit notes invoice-wise, we need the system to calculate the combined total amount across all relevant credit and debit adjustments and then post only one consolidated credit note or debit note against multiple invoices.

For example:

  • Multiple invoices may have credit note and/or debit note adjustments.
  • The system should total all applicable credit and debit values for the customer.
  • Based on the net amount:
    • If the final amount is credit, create one consolidated credit note.
    • If the final amount is debit, create one consolidated debit note.
  • The document should still have reference/linkage to the multiple original invoices.

Business Need:
This is required for specific customers who do not want multiple adjustment documents. They require a single consolidated debit/credit note for all applicable invoices.

Questions for SAP:

  1. Is this requirement supported in standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition / GROW with SAP?
  2. Can one credit memo or debit memo be created with reference to multiple invoices in this scenario?
  3. Is it possible to net both credit and debit adjustments and create only one final credit/debit memo document?
  4. If not available in standard, is there a recommended SAP-approved approach using:
    • Configuration,
    • Billing document split control,
    • Custom logic,
    • Key user extensibility,
    • Side-by-side extension,
    • Or another standard scope item?
  5. Are there any limitations related to billing type, document flow, tax calculation, accounting posting, invoice reference, or audit compliance for this requirement?

Expected Outcome:
whether this requirement is achievable in SAP GROW standard functionality or whether an enhancement/custom extension is required. If possible, please provide the recommended solution approach and relevant configuration/extensibility options.

entry.title: Re: How to Disable payment medium workbench check box wit...

Hi Charan,
The payment files are generated according to the setting in OBPM1/3 and in DMEE/DMEEX this can be refined (you can add more sort/key fields).
The setting in OBPM1 can be refined in OBPM3, more fields can be selected in "Payment medium separated according to level of details'
But if you would like to have less fields flagged for a standard format in OBMP1 I would recommend creating a Z-format and define the levels according to the requirement

It seems to be no problem to set the parameter per server. We are confused about the statistics, we see in the view M_BUFFER_CACHE_STATISTICS. The value of "MAX_SIZE" is still the general value. "ALLOCATED_SIZE" and "USED_SIZE" seems to be reflect a change - which doesn't fit to our settings:

entry.title: V2- The Primary Key of "ITAB" is ignored because it is generic or is the standard key.
entry.content:
I get the message above although I'm following the hints of this message: Solved: The Primary Key of "ITAB" is ignored because it is... - SAP Community. As this message is frozen, I post a new question (V2 for version 2 of the question).

Here's my code:

    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ty_ekpo_key,
        ebeln TYPE ekpo-ebeln,
        ebelp TYPE ekpo-ebelp,
      END OF ty_ekpo_key.
    TYPES tt_ekpo_key TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_ekpo_key WITH UNIQUE KEY table_line.

    DATA(lt_ekpo_key) = VALUE tt_ekpo_key( ).
    SELECT *
      FROM ekpo
           INNER JOIN @LT_ekpo_key AS lt_ekpo_key
             ON  lt_ekpo_key~ebeln = ekpo~ebeln
             AND lt_ekpo_key~ebelp = ekpo~ebelp
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_ekpo).

Thanks.

Sandra

entry.title: Re: V2- The Primary Key of "ITAB" is ignored because it is generic or is the standard key.
entry.content:
The problem is the use of "TABLE_LINE". Although it's technically identical to mentioning explicitly "EBELN EBELP", like the standard key (WITH DEFAULT KEY) also means "EBELN EBELP", that generates the warning message.

So, to avoid the warning message, type this:

    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ty_ekpo_key,
        ebeln TYPE ekpo-ebeln,
        ebelp TYPE ekpo-ebelp,
      END OF ty_ekpo_key.
    TYPES tt_ekpo_key TYPE SORTED TABLE OF ty_ekpo_key WITH UNIQUE KEY ebeln ebelp. "<==== fix

    DATA(lt_ekpo_key) = VALUE tt_ekpo_key( ).
    SELECT *
      FROM ekpo
           INNER JOIN @LT_ekpo_key AS lt_ekpo_key
             ON  lt_ekpo_key~ebeln = ekpo~ebeln
             AND lt_ekpo_key~ebelp = ekpo~ebelp
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_ekpo).

entry.title: UI Composition with Application Frontend Service
entry.content:
As UI application developers, we often find ourselves implementing the same functionality across multiple applications. The natural instinct is to extract that common code into reusable files that can be shared between projects. Traditionally, this is done in one of the following ways:

  • Development time. Copy-pasting shared files directly into each application's source code. This is the easiest way to start, but a maintenance nightmare. Any change to the common logic requires patching and redeploying every application that uses it. On top of that, the duplicated files increase the size of each application and slow down deployments.
  • Build/deploy time. Publishing shared code as an NPM package. This solves the problem of maintaining changes in multiple places, but still requires rebuilding and redeploying all affected applications. It also complicates the release lifecycle, since packages and applications must be released in a specific order.
  • Runtime. Hosting the shared code separately and accessing it via URL:
    • Directly from the client—eliminates code duplication and avoids rebuilding or redeploying applications, but introduces complexity around CORS/CSP restrictions and authentication/session management.
    • Via a server-side proxy—keeps the same user session and avoids CORS/CSP issues, but adds an extra network hop for every request to the shared resources.

All of these approaches work with Application Frontend service. However, in this post I want to show you a more efficient way to achieve UI composition—one that is unique to UIs hosted on Application Frontend service. In short, it is a form of runtime server-side composition, but without the overhead of extra network hops.

To demonstrate the possibilities of application-to-application reuse, we will walk through three common use cases that call for UI composition.

Reusable Application / Component

This use case applies when you have control over the consuming application's source code and want to embed a piece of reusable logic into it. The goal is to benefit from automatic updates to the shared code while preserving the consuming application's URL as the entry point for end users.

Figure 1. Reusable Application

To set this up, the consuming application's xs-app.json needs a route that points to the reusable application. This route uses an "application" property that references another Application Frontend application by name, optionally followed by @ and a specific version. The "source" property contains either a specific resource path or a regular expression that is different from the catch-all (.*) pattern. Path rewriting via the "target" property is also supported. For this use case, the route pointing to the reusable application is placed before the routes pointing to static resources of the consuming application itself.

Here is an example of the consuming application's xs-app.json:

{
  "errorPage": [
    { "status": [404], "path": "/error/404.html" }
  ],
  "routes": [{
    "source": "^/error/(.*)",
    "target": "/common/$1",
    "application": "mylib@1.0.0"
  }, {
    "source": "^(/|/index.html)$",
    "target": "/index.html",
    "service": "app-front"
  }, {
    "source": "/assets/.*",
    "service": "app-front"
  }]
}

This configuration assumes that another Application Frontend application exists with "sap.app/id" set to "mylib" in its manifest.json, deployed in the same BTP subaccount. There is nothing special required in the xs-app.json or manifest.json of the reusable application. For example, if mylib has the following routes:

{
  "routes": [{
    "source": "^/common/(.*)",
    "target": "/dist/$1",
    "service": "app-front"
  }]
}

then a browser request for a resource that does not match any route "source" pattern in the xs-app.json of consuming application (app1) will be redirected to:

https:​//app1-xyzab.eu10.appfront.cloud.sap/error/404.html

and will serve the /dist/404.html file from the mylib application. Any follow-up requests from the error page (for example, to load an illustration image) will also be served from mylib, as long as they use relative paths. This way, you can reuse the same error pages across multiple Application Frontend applications.

Application Extension

This use case applies when you don't have control over—or are not allowed to change—the source code of an existing application, yet you need to create a very similar application with minor modifications. End users will access both the original and the extended application via separate URLs.

Figure 2. Application Extension

The implementation is very similar to the reusable application approach, but inverted: most requests to the extension application serve content from the original application, while the extension itself contains only the delta. In the extension's xs-app.json, the catch-all route points to the original application, and routes for specific paths that need to be overridden are placed before it.

The extension application's xs-app.json might look like this:

{
  "routes": [{
    "source": "^/view/App.view.xml",
    "service": "app-front"
  }, {
    "source": ".*",
    "application": "original"
  }]
}

In this example, the only static resource of extension application (besides xs-app.json and manifest.json) is a modified /view/App.view.xml. This lets you add, remove, or modify parts of the view while inheriting everything else from the original application.

UI Mashup

This use case is about integrating multiple existing UIs and data sources into a single unified application. The mashup has its own URL and behaves as a standalone application from a lifecycle perspective, yet it contains no UI artifacts of its own—only xs-app.json and manifest.json.

This may sound complex at first, but it is essentially a combination of the previous two patterns: the mashup application extends a base application, but instead of hosting override resources itself, it delegates to other applications that act as reusable components.

Figure 3. UI Mashup

Let's look at a concrete example. Imagine a preview application that includes several embedded apps (deployed as part of the preview application itself) and a static apps.json file listing them. The preview application displays whichever embedded app the user selects inside a mobile shell, simulating how the app would look on a real mobile device. Now suppose you want this preview to be able to show any Application Frontend application deployed to your BTP subaccount, with the list of available applications served dynamically from a backend configured in the apps destination. A mashup application solves this.

Consider the following xs-app.json for the mashup application:

{
  "routes": [{
    "source": "^/apps.json",
    "destination": "apps"
  }, {
    "source": "^/app/(.*)",
    "application": "$1"
  }, {
    "source": ".*",
    "application": "preview"
  }]
}

With this configuration, the "application" property in the second route is resolved dynamically based on the request path. The static /apps.json is replaced with dynamic content from the backend, while all other requests fall through to the preview application.

Visibility

The application-to-application reuse relationships are part of Application Frontend application metadata, accessible via the API, CLI, and BTP Cockpit UI (on the application version dependencies screen). Application Frontend service provides not only design-time information about which application depends on which, but also live availability status of all dependencies. This makes it easy to spot issues—such as a missing reusable application or a stopped base application—without digging into the source code or analyzing runtime logs.

Figure 4. BTP Cockpit Application Frontend Dependencies View

Conclusion

The application-to-application reuse feature of Application Frontend service is a powerful mechanism for UI composition that reduces both development and operational costs by improving:

  • Lifecycle management—There is no required order for releasing reusable and consuming applications. Consuming applications do not need to be redeployed to pick up new versions of shared code.
  • Security—No complex CORS/CSP configuration is needed. The browser treats reusable resources as if they were an integral part of the consuming application. A shared user session between consuming and reusable applications ensures consistent sign-out without orphaned "zombie" sessions.
  • Performance—Since all participants in the composition are Application Frontend applications, there are no redundant network calls from the runtime to itself. Application Frontend is aware of all deployed applications and can perform recursive routing target resolution without making extra outbound requests.
  • Extensibility—Base applications do not need to define extensibility points, hooks, or any other upfront preparations. You don't have to touch existing applications. Every Application Frontend application can be reused, extended, or composed into a mashup out of the box.

entry.title: Unfair Result in C_S4CFI_2602 – System-Based Exam Completed but Failed (Request for Review)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I would like to share my experience and seek guidance regarding my recent certification attempt:

Certification: C_S4CFI_2602 – SAP Certified – Implementation Consultant – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting
 
Result: 60.29% (cut score: 61%)
 
During the system-based exam:
- I completed all tasks within the allocated time
- All required processes were executed successfully (journal entries, dunning run, business partner updates, etc.)
- System results confirmed the operations were active and consistent
 
Despite this, I received a failed result. 
I contacted SAP Certification Support, but I was informed that:
- No exam data can be reviewed after submission
- Evaluation is fully automated and cannot be rechecked
 
This raises concerns for me, because:
- This is a system-based exam where execution should be verifiable
- I have evidence that key processes were completed correctly
- The final score is extremely close to the passing threshold
 
Questions to the community:
1. Has anyone experienced a similar issue with system-based exams?
2. Is there any way to escalate beyond standard SAP support?
3. Has anyone successfully received a recredit or review in similar situations?
 
I appreciate any guidance or shared experience.
 
Best regards,
Wellington

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Weinheim - 19th September 2026
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

the last SAP Inside Track in the Rhine-Neckar region was in Walldorf, quite a few years back. We've been missing that kind of day in the region, so we're making one happen. Come and join us on Saturday, 19th September 2026 at Syntax in Weinheim.
 
Date: Saturday, September 19, 2026
Time: 9:00 to 18:00
Location: Syntax Auditorium, Höhnerweg 2-4, 69469 Weinheim
Capacity: 150 seats
Cost: Free
Registration: https://ti.to/sitwhm/sitwhm2026
Website: https://sitwhm.de
 
Important: please register through the link above. Without a registered ticket we can't grant you access to the venue on the day.
 
For anyone who hasn't been to a SIT: it's a free, community-organised event with short talks (20 to 40 minutes), proper breaks, and no commercial agenda. Honestly, the best bits are often the conversations in between the sessions, and that's kind of the point.
 

Call for Speakers

Got something to share? Please send it in on https://sessionize.com/sitwhm2026

Pretty much any SAP topic works: AI and Joule, BTP, ABAP and ABAP Cloud, CAP, UI5 and Fiori, integration, analytics, cloud architecture, clean core, migrations, security, developer experience, side projects, community stuff. If you're into it, chances are others will be too.

First-time speakers, we'd love to have you. Plenty of SIT regulars gave their first ever talk at a SIT.

Session length: 20 or 40 minutes
Language: English only
No product pitches

 

The day before: SAP CodeJam with DJ Adams

Friday, September 18, 9:00 to 16:00, same venue. DJ Adams @qmacro  is doing a full-day CodeJam on CAP CDS modelling. 50 seats. Separate signup at https://ti.to/sitwhm/codejam-whm. Come for one day or both.

 

Friday evening: Pre-event get-together

From 7 PM on Friday we're meeting up somewhere in Weinheim for food and drinks. Casual, no agenda, food and drinks on your own. The venue goes up on sitwhm.de closer to the date. There's a question on the SIT ticket to indicate your interest, please let us know.

 

A small ask

It really helps us if you register early, even if September still feels far away. Makes catering and space planning so much easier on our end.

 

Sponsors and helping out

We're still looking for sponsors, mostly for catering and a few practical things around the day. If that's something your company might be open to, get in touch at team@sitwhm.de or via LinkedIn. Same goes if you want to help on the day itself.

Big thanks to Syntax for hosting us at the auditorium on both days.

 

If you post about it, tag #sitWHM so we can follow along.

See you in Weinheim.

Cheers,
The sitWHM team

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – Easier Than Ever with New Features!
entry.content:
As UX specialists in the SAP S/4HANA Regional Implementation Group and the Fiori Launchpad ABAP team, we often get to work with customers keen to leverage the significant capabilities of SAP Fiori. Are you still using the Fiori Launchpad Designer to maintain your SAP Fiori content? If so, it's time for some good news: migrating to modern tools just got a whole lot easier with SAP S/4HANA 2025.

 

Why This Matters to You

In recent years, SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer has been slowly deprecated and replaced with new tools. Refer to:

  • Setting Up Technical Catalogs with the SAP Fiori Launchpad App Manager
  • SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer: Features Deprecated

With technical catalog migration, you can make your SAP Fiori content future-proof, clean-core compliant - and benefit from improved tools.

 

About this Blog

The tool for technical catalog migration - transaction /UI2/MGR_TC - is available from SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 and is covered in these excellent blogs:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – Why and Getting Ready for Migration​
  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – How the migration process works​

This blog builds on these earlier posts by sharing some exciting news: SAP S/4HANA 2025 brings new features that make technical catalog migration easier and more flexible than ever before. We cover the following:

1. Recap of Technical Catalog Migration
2. The Challenge: What Held Customers Back
3. The Game changer: Partial Migration
4. What Happens Behind the Scenes During a Partial Migration
5. SAP S/4HANA 2025: Partial Migration - Example
6. When To Migrate
7. Frequently Asked Questions
8. Prerequisites & Limitations

Refer to:

  • What’s New in ABAP Platform 2025 FPS01 – Migration of Technical Catalogs
  • What’s New in ABAP Platform 2023 FPS01 – Migration of Technical Catalogs
  • SAP Note: 3170196 - Tools for Setting Up Launchpad Content

1.    Recap of Technical Catalog Migration

In our new launchpad content model, tiles and target mappings are still being used in technical catalogs, but the way of storing them has changed:

  • Previously, tiles and target mappings were loosely coupled.
  • Now, with Launchpad App Descriptor Items (LADIs), they are closely coupled as a pair.

To make your launchpad content future-proof, technical catalogs created with the deprecated SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer must be migrated to the SAP Fiori Launchpad App Manager (transaction /UI2/FLPAM).

Tiles and target mappings / LADIs

  • Technical catalogs created in the deprecated Fiori Launchpad Designer:

           NOTE: Technical catalogs created in the Fiori Launchpad Designer are only are visible in App Manager, only after migration.

  • Migrated technical catalogs and catalogs created in Launchpad App Manager:

          Now the technical catalogs are maintained in App Manager. They are also visible (read-only) in the Fiori Launchpad Designer

 

Migrated technical catalog

 

2.  The Challenge: What Held Customers Back

Until now, the migration tool required that your technical catalogs are perfect before migration. If a catalog had some apps that could be migrated and some that couldn't, the entire catalog was marked as "Not Ready for Migration." This meant you had to clean up everything before you could start the migration. The migration tool has always provided detailed error descriptions and suggested fixes, but fixing all errors could still be a time-consuming process.

Here's what could block migration:

  • Tiles or target mappings with configuration errors
  • Mixed catalogs, containing originals and references
  • Catalog names outside the customer namespace for workbench objects
  • Content not following best practices 

3. The Gamechanger: Partial Migration

With SAP S/4HANA 2025, we have introduced a feature that makes your life easier: partial catalog migration.

Now you can migrate the apps that are ready, while keeping the problematic ones in the old format temporarily. Affected catalogs have the status: "1c Ready for Partial Migration."

Additional bonus: if your old catalog name doesn't meet current naming requirements, you can correct the name during migration. The tool automatically updates all references, so nothing breaks in the process.     

Partial migration

 Note: The partial migration transfers all your translations automatically – as long as they're in the same system. Maintaining translations separately? You have two options:                                                                                    

  • Retranslate the content in the new Fiori Launchpad App Manager-based catalog after partial migration, or                         
  • Clean up your technical catalogs first and use the full migration path available from SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01. 

What this means

You're no longer blocked! Migrate what you can today and handle the exceptions separately. Apps that can't be migrated stay in the old catalog, while everything else moves to the modern tools where you can maintain them more easily.

Already have clean catalogs that follow best practices? No problem! The 'Full Migration' option from SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 is still fully supported in 2025. Refer to the blogs below:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – Why and Getting Ready for Migration​
  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – How the migration process works

4. What Happens Behind the Scenes During a Partial Migration

Before migration: Let’s look at a technical catalog Z_OLD_CATALOG_TC (created in the Fiori Launchpad Designer) that stores both original and reference tiles and target mappings (i.e. a mixed catalog).:

 

During migration: This is shown below in steps 1 and 2.

Step 1: Original tile and original target mapping are combined into a newly created LADI (launchpad app descriptor item) in an existing or new Launchpad App Manager based catalog Z_NEW_CATALOG_TC.

Step 2: In the old catalog Z_OLD_CATALOG_TC, convert the Fiori Launchpad Designer based original tile and target mapping into references pointing towards the newly created LADI from step 1. This prevents the broken references in our launchpad content. (Note: The picture below shows a different catalog name, where special characters have been replaced with compliant characters according to naming conventions.)

Before / after migration

 

After the migration: The new catalog Z_NEW_CATALOG_TC can be seen in the SAP Fiori Launchpad Application Manager

After migration

The Fiori Launchpad Designer based catalog Z_OLD_CATALOG still exists and may have the following statuses:

- "Not Ready" – This happens if some tiles/target mappings in the catalog could not be migrated.

  • Reference tiles and target mappings are the same as before the migration.
  • Originals that could be migrated have become references.
  • Original apps that do not conform to the requirements in  SAP Note: 3397026 - SAP Fiori Launchpad - Technical Catalog Migration - Content Cleanup Risks stay as originals. These cannot be migrated without manual cleanup activities. Check their status for detailed errors. If you still need these apps, clean them up and migrate them automatically. Alternative: if you recreate the apps manually in App Manager, make sure to adjust your Business Catalogs to point to the new apps.

- "Not Relevant" - all originals have successfully been migrated and only references are left (known as Business Catalogs)

Summary: You're no longer blocked by unmovable apps. Migrate what you can and handle the rest separately!

 

5. SAP S/4HANA 2025: Partial Migration - Example

Let’s look at a real-life catalog that is not perfect and requires partial migration, ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_TC. Besides some originals, it also contains a reference, and it needs assignments of tiles to target mappings.  

  • Migratable:  
    • Intent DevelopmentObject-translate
    • Tile only
  • Migratable, but manual tile-target mapping assignment required:
    • Intent DevelopmentObject-connect: 1 tile matching 2 target mappings
    • Intent DevelopmentObject-map: 2 tiles matching 1 target mapping
  • Reference, no need to migrate:  
    • Intent FLPBusinessCatalog-manageCrossClient

 Partial migration steps

1. Run transaction /UI2/MGR_TC, enter your technical catalog name (ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_TC here) and click on 'Execute':

Run transaction /UI2/MGR_TC

 NOTE: The selection screen of transaction /UI2/MGR_TC works as a filter to show only the technical catalogs that you want to work with. You can use ‘Z*’ (or your naming convention) to get an overview of your custom technical catalogs.

2. Select Migration Status as '1c Ready for Partial Migration':

Status 'Ready for Partial Migration''

3. Now you can see the technical catalog ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_TC, along with a list of statuses for each tile / target mapping.

 4. Select the technical catalog (in this case ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_TC) and click 'Start Partial Migration'.

Start Partial Migration

 5. Here, we have selected to create a new technical catalog ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_MIGRATED_TC. Choose an existing catalog or create a new one and click on the green tick.

NOTE: Please follow your naming conventions for technical catalogs, you do not need to use a keyword like 'MIGRATED', it is used here purely for illustration purposes.

Select technical catalog6. Depending on the content of the catalog, you see a screen for pairing tiles and target mappings. Normally there is one tile matching to one target mapping with the same semantic object / action and parameters, in this case the tile and target mapping assignment screen is skipped.

If there is more than one tile matching a target mapping or vice versa, like for our example catalog, user interaction is required.

What you see:

  • A list of intents (on the left) that needs assignment or are missing a default tile
  • Only intents with unclear tile-to-target-mapping connections are shown by default. Click ‘Show All Intents’ in the toolbar to include intents that could be resolved automatically.

 Key difference from before migration and after migration:

  • Before (in the Fiori Launchpad Designer): Tiles and target mappings are loosely connected via matching intents.
  • After (in Launchpad App Manager): Tiles and target mappings are coupled entities with the target mapping taking the lead.

 Steps you need to complete:

            a) Process all intents marked Assignment Needed (marked with a red 'X' in the status column below). See Step 6.1

            b) Assign each tile to a matching target mapping. See Step 6.2

            c) If multiple tiles match one target mapping, select which tile is the default. (Any additional tiles will be labelled as "Additional Tiles".).See Step 6.3

             d) Click Start Partial Migration. See Step 6.4

6.1 Click on the first intent in error (in the example, it’s DevelopmentObject-connect, with 2 target mappings matching 1 tile) The status text of the tile tells you to ‘Assign to target mapping’, so select the correct target mapping and click on Assign to Target Mapping.

 6.2. Now you see a green tick for both the intent and for the tile: tile and target mapping have been paired, and all uncertainties have been resolved.

 6.3 Now select the remaining intent, DevelopmentObject-map (1 target mapping matching 2 tiles) and the status text of the target mapping tells you to ‘Set default tile’. Select the correct tile and click on Set as Default Tile.

6.4 Now you can see that all intents have the green tick and that a Default Tile has been chosen. Click on Start Partial Migration:

7. When prompted, enter a suitable development package and select a transport (if required). In this example, we are saving the new standard technical catalog as a Local Object (Package = $TMP).

 8. Now you can see the migration of the technical catalog to ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_MIGRATED_TC has been completed successfully. It is displayed in the list of catalogs with status ‘4 Migration Finished’. Select the technical catalog and click on Open SAP Fiori Launchpad App Manager.

 9. Your newly created technical catalog ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_MIGRATED_TC is displayed in SAP Fiori Launchpad App Manager, where you can view the corresponding LADIs.

10.  The old Fiori Launchpad Designer Catalog ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_TC is now in status 'Not Relevant for Migration'. It is now a business catalog that contains only references. Below you can see this in both the migration tool (transaction /UI2/MGR_TC) and the Fiori Launchpad Designer:

 

Consider the deletion of the old Fiori Launchpad Designer technical catalog if you are sure that it is not used anymore. 

11. For the end user, the Fiori launchpad will look the same, before and after migration. Below, you can see a sample screenshot of how this new technical catalog ZCL_MGR_EXAMPLE_02_MIGRATED_TC looks in the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

6. When To Migrate

If you’re already on release SAP S/4HANA 2025, it’s easy: migrate as soon as possible.

 If you’re on release SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01+, it depends on your content. Run transaction /UI2/MGR_TC to check if any catalogs need special attention and what issues need to be resolved:

  • Are all catalogs in status ‘Ready for Migration’, or ‘Ready for Migration – Assignment Required’? Great. Migrate on SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01.
  • Are some of your catalogs in status ‘Not Ready for Migration’? Check the list of migration issues to see how much manual rework would be required. You can minimize the effort by upgrading first and running the migration on SAP S/4HANA 2025.
  • Do you need to rename your technical catalogs? This is only possible with the partial migration in release SAP S/4HANA 2025. Upgrade first and run the migration on SAP S/4HANA 2025.

 7. Frequently Asked Questions

Question: Can I migrate client-specific catalogs?

- Answer: Yes. Note that migration will transform them into cross-client catalogs, for better reuse and because the resulting launchpad app descriptor items are cross-client workbench objects.

Question: Does the technical catalog migration provide any options for moving cross-client catalogs to the client-specific layer?

- Answer: No. Cross-client and client-specific technical catalogs are always moved to cross-client. This is because LADIs are always cross-client.

Question: Do I have to change my authorizations after upgrade?

- Answer: No authorization changes are required. References and links in your business catalogs stay intact, no rework is required

Question: Can admins see the new technical catalog in the old Fiori Launchpad Designer after migration?

- Answer: Yes, admins can see the new technical catalog in the old Fiori Launchpad Designer, but only as read-only (they can view it but not change it). To edit the technical catalog, administrators must use Launchpad App Manager tool instead.

Question: What object directory entries are involved?

Answer:

  • Technical catalogs created in the Fiori Launchpad Designer - Object Directory Entry R3TR / UIAC
  • Technical catalogs that have been migrated have Object Directory Entry R3TR / UIAD

After migration, SAP recommends using the following tools:

  • Launchpad App Manager – for technical catalogs
  • Launchpad Content Manager – for business catalogs

8. Prerequisites & Limitations

 Question: Are there any prerequisites, or any limitations concerning the content that can be migrated with release SAP S/4HANA 2025?

  • Check SAP 3397026 - SAP Fiori Launchpad - Technical Catalog Migration - Content Cleanup Risks for prerequisites.
  • Apps with system aliases other than LOCAL are not supported in Launchpad App Manager yet.

Recommendation: Keep target mappings with other system aliases (plus tiles with the matching intent) in the deprecated Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  • Custom tiles are in maintenance mode. They are not supported in Launchpad App Manager, only by the deprecated Fiori Launchpad Designer.

Recommendation: Consider replacing your custom tiles with standard tiles. If that is not possible, keep your custom tiles in the deprecated Fiori Launchpad Designer. Even if some tiles and target mappings need to stay in the deprecated Fiori Launchpad Designer, SAP recommends that you migrate the rest of the catalog using partial migration.

 Resources worth knowing:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – Why and Getting Ready for Migration​
  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - Technical Catalog Migration – How the migration process works​
  • What’s New in ABAP Platform 2025 FPS00 – Migration of technical catalogs
  • What’s New in ABAP Platform 2023 FPS01 – Migration of technical catalogs
  • SAP Note: 3170196 - Tools for Setting Up Launchpad Content
  • SAP Note: 3397026 - SAP Fiori Launchpad - technical catalog Migration - Content Cleanup Risks

You'll find much more on the community topic page for SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

 Other helpful links in the SAP Community:

  • Follow our tag S4HANA RIG - SAP Community for more from the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG and the Fiori Launchpad ABAP Team.

entry.title: KPI indicatorDataSource routing error: Service targeting <destination-rt> instead of <destination-sr
entry.content:
Hello,

I am facing an issue while configuring a dynamic KPI tile in SAP Build Work Zone. In my project, the site is deployed using CDM (cdm.json) to define the site structure, apps, and tiles. The KPI is configured in the application manifest.json through indicatorDataSource, as described in the SAP documentation and this blog :

 

 

 

The tile shows "... Error" and the request fails with a 500 Internal Server Error. From the browser Network tab, I can see that the KPI request is routed through a URL like: ".../dynamic_dest/<destination-rt>/<AppID>/~/odata/...".
Here, <destination-rt> is the destination that points to the HTML5 Application Repository runtime. However, the OData service used for the KPI is exposed by my CAP backend service (<destination-srv-api>), not by the HTML5 app runtime. If I call the backend OData endpoint directly (by removing the "/dynamic_dest/<destination-rt>" part of the URL), the service responds correctly.

entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Accessing PODs 2.0
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

New POD 2.0 Framework

  1. The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. Visual WYSIWYG Designer
  4. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 1
  5. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 2
  6. Custom UI Dialogs and Calling Production Processes
  7. Dynamic PODs, How to Dynamically Show, Hide, Enable, and Disable UI Elements
  8. Other Business Capabilities Enabled by POD 2.0
  9. Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)
  10. POD Notifications
  11. Tips and Tricks
  12. Accessing PODs 2.0

Accessing PODs 2.0

PODs created using the brand-new POD 2.0 framework can be accessed via:

  • Launch PODs app
  • Bookmark URLs
  • Desktop Shortcuts

For Launch PODs app, 1st assign PODs 2.0 to Users in Manage PODs 2.0:

Then, use Launch PODs app to launch them:

For Bookmark URLs, just bookmark the POD 2.0 URL, and launch it from the bookmark:

For example, in Google Chrome:

For Desktop Shortcuts:

  1. On your computer, open Google Chrome.
  2. Go to the POD 2.0 you want to create a shortcut for.
  3. At the top right, select More and then Cast, save, and share and then Create shortcut.
  4. From the dialog that appears:
  5. Name: Select the default name for the shortcut or rename it.
  6. Select Create.

A Desktop shortcut is created from where you can launch it.

Or simply ‘Run’ (click the ‘Play’ button) directly from Manage PODs 2.0 app.

Have you tried POD 2.0 already? Let me know in the comments!

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Re: Interest calcuation on Customer Open Items with 30/60/90 grace days
entry.content:
Hello Purandar, if you are using the Contract Accounts Receivables and Payables subledger (FI-CA) you may set the number of Transfer Days in the Define Interest Key configuration activity. Transfer days are added to the due date for net payment in a receivable. This is also the source of the baseline date for interest calculation. Baseline date for interest calculation = due date for net payment + transfer days.

This setting is not valid for the FI-AR subledger.  

Best regards

Sabine

Thank you for reaching out

At this time, we are unfortunately unable to provide an immediate fix; however, please rest assured that this issue is being treated as a priority.

entry.title: Re: Custom Domain Service in SAP BTP Build Work Zone (Standard/Advanced Edition)
entry.content:
Hi @Farinha1988 

You won't able to use the existing certificate which is generated already by your company because it will be generated through different CSR.

I also faced this issue and after lot of reserach and confirmation from SAP Core team, I got to know this.

 

Please generate the new CSR from the Custom Domain Service (either SAN or Wild Card) and give that CSR to Certificate Authority to sign and it will work.

 

I have mentioned this thing already in the Important Notes section of my blog.

 

Please let me know if you face any challange.

 

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
There’s a lot happening around SAP Business Data Cloud right now, and honestly, many partners are asking the same question: where do we start when it comes to architecture guidance?

That’s exactly why we’re launching the “Leveraging SAP Architecture Center for SAP Business Data Cloud” session as part of our new data architecture miniseries for SAP Partners.

The SAP Architecture Center brings together reference architectures, solution diagrams, component insights, and practical examples that help teams move faster and align around proven approaches.  Plus, SAP Business Data Cloud sits right at the center of many of today’s top data and AI conversations.

In this session, we’ll walk through real use cases including SAP BW modernization, SAP Databricks integration, enterprise data strategy, and AI scenarios for SAP BDC.  You’ll also see how these resources fit into existing architect toolchains like draw.io and how partners can actively contribute.

If you work in architecture, sales, delivery, advisory, or innovation, this session was built with you in mind.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010833.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPBusinessDataCloud #EnterpriseArchitecture #DataStrategy #BusinessAI

entry.title: Re: TU transaction in SAP EWM S4/HANA
entry.content:
Hello,

Here is the explanation of this behaviour: https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/sewm/5007031381.html?q=/scwm/tu+default 

 

Shipping Overviews

Time horizon filter in Transport Unit Overview Outb. and Transport Unit Overview Inb. nodes works according to the following logic:

If you set a time horizon, but there is a TU still active in the system, it will be shown even if its dates are set outside of the time frame, because at the moment of the search it is in active status in the system, so the warehouse administrator should be aware of it. The result is the same even if Reduced Selection flag is checked. Planned and completed TUs will be filtered out as expected.

E.g. TU is in active status with

  • Assignment Start Date = 01.01.2021
  • Assignment End Date = 05.01.2021

and you filter for time horizon between 01.11.2024 and 30.11.2024, this TU will be found and shown in the result.

 

BR Bünyamin

entry.title: Re: Issue with I_OrganizationAddress Not Available for Ex...
entry.content:
Dear @habeeb_unissa,

This is expected behavior in Public Cloud because I_OrganizationAddress is a released CDS view and can be used in Custom CDS View modelling but that does not automatically mean it is eligible as a data source when creating a Custom CDS View with scenario External API. SAP applies scenario-specific eligibility checks. SAP describes this exact pattern the view can be Released in View Browser but still not selectable when creating an External API CDS view in such cases the usage is restricted by the CDS view’s supported scenario/capability, not by missing configuration

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003495724

For I_OrganizationAddress, SAP documents it as the CDS view Address of an Organization, used to return organization name, postal address and communication data. The same SAP documentation shows this view under the CDS view documentation with data extraction-related information and remote-use stability information but this is not the same as being selectable as a source for a key-user External API OData view

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/4af1b5dd2fab4ed09bf7e433819e4226.html

Supported options:

1. Create a normal Custom CDS View and use I_OrganizationAddress as an associated/joined data source. SAP’s own guidance for company code address extraction uses this pattern primary data source I_CompanyCode, association/join to I_OrganizationAddress via AddressID then select address fields such as street, city, postal code, region, country, phone and email

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003724443

2. Use only CDS data sources that are available in the External API scenario then expose the custom CDS view through a Custom Communication Scenario and Communication Arrangement. SAP documents this as the standard process for exposing custom CDS views as external APIs

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/f4dcfd6e14b54177b98d6099ddb2014e.html

https://learning.sap.com/videos/exposing-custom-cds-views-as-external-apis-sap-s-4hana-cloud-extensibility

3. If I_OrganizationAddress specifically must be exposed as an External API source then correct route is a feature request / SAP influence request, because SAP would need to extend the supported capabilities of the delivered CDS view for this scenario. SAP states the same resolution for CDS views that are released but not available as data sources in the External API scenario

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003495724

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002963059

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Issue Maintaining 19-Digit Bank Account Number for Customer Master Data in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We are facing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud while maintaining customer bank details through the “Manage Business Partner Master Data” / "Maintain Business Partner" app.

The customer has a 19-digit bank account number, however, the system currently allows entry of only up to 18 digits for the bank account number field.

To resolve this, we tried updating the bank account number length from 18 to 19 in the country-specific settings (attached image for reference), but the change is not taking effect and the system is still restricting the entry to 18 digits.

Could you please suggest:

  1. Is it possible in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to maintain a bank account number with more than 18 digits in the Business Partner app?
  2. Is there any configuration, SSCUI, or workaround available to achieve this requirement?
  3. Are there any SAP notes or standard limitations related to this behavior?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

 

Thanks and Regards,

Rohini Dutta
